name: Muriel de Zapardiel and the Ruins of Honquilana Loop
name_original: Ruinas de Honquilana desde Muriel de Zapardiel
description: Hike 8.2 miles (13.1 km) from Muriel de Zapardiel to the abandoned village of Honquilana, exploring historical ruins and rural landscapes.
region_intro: Hiking around Sur, located in the province of Valladolid, Spain, offers a diverse landscape primarily characterized by river valleys, agricultural plains, and gentle hills. The region features trails along the Duero and Adaja rivers, passing through vineyards, cork oak forests, and almond groves. This area provides a mix of natural beauty and cultural landmarks, with routes connecting historic towns and rural settings. Best hiking trails around Sur The most popular hiking route is Almond Tree Trail, a 3.0 miles (4.9 km) trail that takes 1 hour 22 minutes to complete. This easy path winds through picturesque almond groves. Another top favourite among local hikers is Foncastín Cork Oak Trail, a moderate 5.8 miles (9.3 km) path. This route leads through a notable cork oak forest, offering varied terrain. Local hikers also love the Tordesillas and Duero Trail Loop, a 6.3 miles (10.1 km) trail leading through riverside landscapes and historical areas, often completed in about 2 hours 37 minutes. Hiking around Sur is defined by river paths, agricultural fields, and gentle hills, with routes suitable for various fitness levels. The network offers options for both leisurely walks and more extended excursions. The routes in Sur are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.6 stars from over 50 reviews. More than 400 hikers have used komoot to explore Sur's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are available around Sur, Spain?
A: There are over 50 hiking trails available around Sur, Spain, offering a variety of experiences. The majority of these routes are classified as easy or moderate, making them accessible for many hikers.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect when hiking near Sur?
A: Hiking near Sur, in the province of Valladolid, offers a diverse landscape characterized by serene river valleys, expansive agricultural plains, and gentle rolling hills. You'll find trails winding along the Duero and Adaja rivers, passing through picturesque vineyards, cork oak forests, and charming almond groves.

Q: Are there any easy, family-friendly hikes in the Sur area?
A: Yes, Sur offers several easy trails perfect for families. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1080212954/almond-tree-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Almond Tree Trail&lt;/a&gt;, which is 4.9 km long and takes about 1 hour 22 minutes to complete, winding through beautiful almond groves.

Q: Can I find circular hiking routes around Sur?
A: Absolutely. Many routes in the region are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084910405/tordesillas-and-duero-trail-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tordesillas and Duero Trail Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a 10.1 km journey through riverside landscapes and historical areas.

Q: What do other hikers enjoy most about the trails in Sur?
A: The trails in Sur are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.6 stars. Hikers frequently praise the peaceful river paths, the beauty of the agricultural fields, and the well-maintained routes that offer a pleasant mix of natural beauty and cultural landmarks.

Q: Are there any trails that explore the unique cork oak forests mentioned in the region?
A: Yes, you can experience the distinctive cork oak forests on trails like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084965040/foncastin-cork-oak-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Foncastín Cork Oak Trail&lt;/a&gt;. This moderate 9.3 km path leads you directly through one of these notable forests, offering varied terrain and a unique natural environment.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or points of interest I can see along the hiking trails near Sur?
A: Many trails in the Sur area connect natural beauty with historical sites. You can explore the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5269241" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Historic Center of Tordesillas&lt;/a&gt;, or discover religious buildings like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5834417" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Santa María la Mayor de la Asunción de Tordesillas&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5621377" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Collegiate Church of San Antolín&lt;/a&gt;. Some routes also pass by the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5509532" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;San José Dam&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any longer, more challenging routes for experienced hikers?
A: While most trails are easy to moderate, there are longer options for those seeking a more extended excursion. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1345320068/villanueva-de-duero-from-valdestillas-circular-by-the-rio-adaja" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de Duero from Valdestillas — circular by the Río Adaja&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 18.8 km route that provides a longer journey through the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Can I find trails that specifically follow the Duero or Adaja rivers?
A: Yes, the region is defined by its river valleys. You can find routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1092321836/villafranca-de-duero-on-the-banks-of-the-duero-river-castronuno-riverside-nature-reserve" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villafranca de Duero on the banks of the Duero River&lt;/a&gt;, which takes you along the riverside, often through the Castronuño Riverside Nature Reserve, offering beautiful water views and natural habitats.

Q: Are there any trails that showcase the agricultural side of the Sur region, such as vineyards?
A: Indeed. The agricultural plains and vineyards are a significant feature of the Sur landscape. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1084981934/between-the-vineyards-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Between the Vineyards Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 9.3 km route specifically designed to take you through these scenic wine-growing areas.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Sur?
A: Given the region's climate, spring and autumn are generally ideal for hiking in Sur. The temperatures are milder, and the landscapes are particularly vibrant, with blooming almond trees in spring or rich autumnal colors in the vineyards and forests.
tips: Catholic sanctuary in the town of Muriel de Zapardiel in Valladolid. This church is dated between the 11th and 12th centuries and the fact that the tower is separated from the church is striking.
Honquilana is a depopulated Valladolid now in ruins although it had housed 20 inhabitants in the mid-19th century.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail to the Ruins of Honquilana?
faqs.answer: The journey to the Ruins of Honquilana typically starts from Muriel de Zapardiel. While specific parking areas aren't formally designated for the trail, you can usually find parking within the town of Muriel de Zapardiel, which serves as the primary access point for this route.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginners or families?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is generally considered to be of low to medium difficulty, making it accessible for most hikers, including beginners. The terrain is mostly flat or gently rolling, typical of the Castilian plain. It's a good option for families looking for a walk with a unique historical destination.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on the way to Honquilana?
faqs.answer: The terrain primarily consists of open fields and pine forests, with some paths running near rivers like the Zapardiel and Adaja. It's generally flat or gently rolling agricultural land and woodland. Be aware that after rainfall, some areas might be damp or muddy.

faqs.question: What makes the Ruins of Honquilana a notable destination?
faqs.answer: The primary draw of this route is the destination itself: the Ruins of Honquilana. This abandoned village, often called a 'ghost town,' offers a poignant glimpse into Spain's rural past. Its last inhabitant left in the mid-1980s, leaving behind a place of significant historical interest and eerie charm, dating back to at least 1250.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to visit the ruins?
faqs.answer: The route can be enjoyed throughout the year, but spring and autumn offer the most pleasant conditions with milder temperatures. During these seasons, the landscape is also particularly beautiful. Summers can be hot, and winters might bring colder, potentially wet, conditions.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on this trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, dogs are generally allowed on rural trails in the Valladolid province, including this route. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near wildlife, and to ensure you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to visit the abandoned village?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to hike this route or to visit the Ruins of Honquilana. It's an open, accessible rural area.

faqs.question: What kind of wildlife might I see along the route?
faqs.answer: The area is home to various wildlife, typical of the Castilian plain. You might spot partridges and hares, among other local species, especially in the quieter sections of the route through fields and woodlands.

faqs.question: Are there any other points of interest besides the ruins?
faqs.answer: Yes, in Muriel de Zapardiel, the starting point, you can visit the impressive Church of Nuestra Señora del Castillo, which showcases Romanesque-Mudéjar architecture. The village also has a medieval necropolis, reflecting the rich history of the region.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with any other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, sections of this route overlap with other established paths. Approximately 30% of the journey follows parts of the Camino de Santiago del Sureste and Etapa 24: Arévalo - Medina del Campo. You'll also encounter smaller local paths like Calle Cañada and Camino las Bodegas.

faqs.question: Are there any facilities like cafes or restrooms near the trailhead?
faqs.answer: Muriel de Zapardiel, being the starting point, is a small town where you might find basic amenities such as a local bar or cafe. It's advisable to check opening hours, especially in smaller villages. There are no facilities directly on the trail itself, so plan accordingly.

waypoints.name: Church of Nuestra Señora del Castillo
